question: A ball of mass $0.2\,kg$ is thrown from a height of $1\,m$ and with an initial velocity of $\sqrt{10} {~m} / {s}$ at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Assuming, acceleration due to gravity $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, then modulus of momentum increment during the total time of motion in ${kg} {m} / {s}$ is
explanation:
There is no change in velocity in $X$ direction, so momentum change occurs only in $Y$-direction.
Now initial momentum in $Y$-direction is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952135/preptracker/questions/q_107324_explanation_1764952135210.png $\vec{p}_{y}($ initial) $=m \vec{v}_{y}=0.2\left(\sqrt{10} \sin 45^{\circ} \hat{j}\right) k g-m s^{-1}$
$=0.2 \times \sqrt{10} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \hat{j} {~kg}-m s^{-1}$
$=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \cdot \hat{j} k g-m s^{-1}$
Final momentum in $Y$-direction is
$\vec{p}_{y(\text { final })}=m \vec{v}_{y}=m\left(\sqrt{u_{y}^{2}+2 g h}\right)(-\hat{j})$
$=\left(0.2 \sqrt{\left(\sqrt{5}^{2}\right)+2(-10)(-1)}\right)(-\hat{j}) k g-m s^{-1}$
$=-1 \hat{j} {~kg}-{ms}^{-1}$ (downwards)
So, change in momentum
$\Delta \vec{p}=\vec{p}_{y(\text { final })}-\vec{p}_{y(\text { initial })}=-1 \hat{j}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \hat{j}$
$=-\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}\right) \hat{j} k g-m s^{-1}$
$|\Delta \vec{p}|=1+\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}=\dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{\sqrt{5}}$
So,the correct option is (3)
subject: Physics

question: Two objects are located at height $10 m$ above the ground. At some point of time, the objects are thrown with initial velocity $2 \sqrt{2} {~m} / {s}$ at an angle $45^{\circ}$ and $135^{\circ}$ with the positive $X$-axis, respectively. Assuming $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, the velocity vectors will be perpendicular to each other at time is equal to
explanation:
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952132/preptracker/questions/q_107319_explanation_1764952131898.png In this projectile motion, both objects are projected at the angle difference of $90^{\circ}$. They will be again have same angle difference, when they are again at same height (of $10\,m$) from ground. Taking, plane ${O}_{2} {OO}_{1}$ as reference, time taken to reach at point $O_{1}$ by first object is
Time of flight, $T_{1}=\dfrac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}=\dfrac{2 \times 2 \sqrt{2} \sin 45^{\circ}}{10}$
$=0.4 {~s}$
Time taken by second object to reach at point ${O}_{2}$ is
$T_{2}=\dfrac{2 \times 2 \sqrt{2} \sin 135^{\circ}}{10}=0.4 {~s}$
$\because \quad T_{1}=T_{2}$
So, velocities of both objects will be perpendicular to each other after $0.4\,s$.
So, the correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A stone projected from the ground with a velocity of $50 {~ms}^{-1}$ at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal crosses a wall after a time of $3 s$. Then the horizontal distance beyond the wall that the stone strikes the ground is (acceleration due to gravity $=10 {~ms}^{-2}$ )
explanation:
Given, initial velocity $(u)=50 m s^{-1}$
angle of projection, $(\theta)=30^{\circ}$
Horizontal distance covered in $3 s$,
$x=50 \cos 30^{\circ} \times 3 m$
$=50 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 3 {~m}$
$=129.9 {~m}$
Range, $R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}=\dfrac{2500 \times\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)}{10} m$
$=216.5 {~m}$
Now, we have time $t=3 s$
$\dfrac{t}{T}=\dfrac{x}{R} \Rightarrow \dfrac{3}{5}=\dfrac{x}{216.5}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=\dfrac{3}{5} \times 216.5=129.9 {~m}$
The distance beyond the well where the stone will hit,
$=R-x=216.5-129.9=86.6 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A player can throw a ball to a maximum horizontal distance of $80\,m$. If he throws the ball vertically with the same velocity, then the maximum height attained by the ball is
explanation:
Using the formula for range,$R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
For max range: $R_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}$
$\Rightarrow 80 \times 10=u^{2} \quad\left[g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right]$
Now using $3{ }^{\text {rd }}$ equation of motion for vertical motion,
$v^{2}=u^{2}-2 a s$ (direction of $u$ and $g$ is opposite in nature) at highest point $v=0$
$u^{2}=2 g s_{\text {max }} \Rightarrow s_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{u^{2}}{2 g}$
( $s$ is height from horizontal level)
$\therefore s_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{800}{2 \times 10}=40 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is given an initial velocity of $\hat{i}+2 \hat{j} {~ms}^{-1}$. The cartesian equation of its path is ( $x$ and $y$ are in metres and $g=10 {~ms}^{-1}$ )
explanation:
Given, initial velocity, $\vec{u}=\hat{{i}}+2 \hat{{j}} m s^{-1}$
Acceleration due to gravity $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952128/preptracker/questions/q_107311_explanation_1764952128450.png $\tan \theta=\dfrac{2}{1} \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$
Using the formula for path equation
$y=x \tan \theta-\dfrac{g x^{2}}{2 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}$
$|\vec{u}|=\sqrt{1^{2}+2^{2}}=\sqrt{5} {~m} / {s}$
$y=2 x-\dfrac{10 \times x^{2}}{2 \times 5 \times \dfrac{1}{5}} \Rightarrow y=2 x-5 x^{2}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is launched with an initial speed of $40 {~m} / {s}$ at an angle $30^{\circ}$ above the ground. The projectile lands on a hillside $2.0 s$ later. The net displacement from where the projectile was launched to where it hits the target is (take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957784/preptracker/questions/q_107310_explanation_1764957784542.png Since, time of flight, $T=\dfrac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}$
$=\dfrac{2 \times 40 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{10}=\dfrac{2 \times 40 \times 1}{10 \times 2}=4 s$
$\therefore$ In $2 s$, it will be at the topmost point, where it will hit the hillside
After $2 s$, the maximum height ( $H_{\text {max }}$ )
$A B=H_{\text {max }}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}=\dfrac{40^{2} \sin ^{2} 30^{\circ}}{2 \times 10} m$
$=\dfrac{1600 \times 1}{2 \times 10 \times 4}=20 {~m}$
Also, at this time the horizontal distance travelled will be half of the range $=O A$ i.e $\dfrac{R}{2}$$O A=\dfrac{R}{2}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{2 g}=\dfrac{40^{2} \sin \left(2 \times 30^{\circ}\right)}{2 \times 10} m$
$=\dfrac{1600 \sin 60^{\circ}}{20} m$
$\therefore \dfrac{R}{2}=80 \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=40 \sqrt{3} {~m}$
The displacement from the projection point,$O B=D=\sqrt{O A^{2}+A B^{2}}$
(By Pythagorous theorem)
$\Rightarrow D=\sqrt{(40 \sqrt{3})^{2}+(20)^{2}}=\sqrt{4800+400}$
$=\sqrt{5200} {~m}=20 \sqrt{13} {~m}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is given an initial velocity of $(3 \hat{i}+4 \hat{j}) {m} / {s}$ where $\hat{i}$ is along the ground and $\hat{j}$ is along the vertical. Assuming $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$, if the equation of its trajectory can be written as $\dfrac{1}{9}\left[\beta x+\gamma x^{2}\right]$. Then the value of $\gamma$ is
explanation:
Initial velocity,
$\vec{u}=(3 \hat{{i}}+4 \hat{{j}}) {m} / {s}|\vec{u}|=\sqrt{3^{2}+4^{2}}=5 {~m} / {s}$
$\tan \theta=4 / 3$
$\therefore \theta=53^{\circ}=$ Angle of projection
We know that, equation of trajectory of a projectile is
$y=x \tan \theta-\dfrac{g x^{2}}{2 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}$
On Comparing with $y=\dfrac{1}{9} \beta x+\dfrac{\gamma}{9} x^{2}$
We find that, $\dfrac{\beta}{9}=\tan \theta$ and $\dfrac{\gamma}{9}=\dfrac{-g}{2 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\beta}{9}=\tan 53^{\circ}$ and $\dfrac{\gamma}{9}=\dfrac{-10}{2(5)^{2} \cos ^{2} 53^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow \beta=9 \times \dfrac{4}{3}=12$ and $\dfrac{\gamma}{9}=\dfrac{-5}{25 \times\left(\dfrac{3}{5}\right)^{2}} \Rightarrow \gamma=-5$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: For a projectile, if $\alpha$ is the angle of projection, $R$ is the range, $h$ is the maximum height, $t$ is the time of flight then
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957781/preptracker/questions/q_107304_explanation_1764957781422.png For a projectile,Range, $R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \alpha}{g}$
Maximum height, $h=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha}{2 g}$
Time of flight, $t=\dfrac{2 u \sin \alpha}{g}$
Now, $\dfrac{h}{R}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha / 2 g}{u^{2} \sin 2 \alpha / g}=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} \alpha}{2 \sin 2 \alpha}$$=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} \alpha}{2 \cdot 2 \sin \alpha \cos \alpha}$
$(\because \sin 2 \alpha=2 \sin \alpha \cdot \cos \alpha)$
$=\dfrac{1}{4} \tan \alpha \Rightarrow \tan \alpha=\dfrac{4 h}{R}$
Also, $\dfrac{h}{t^{2}}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha / 2 g}{4 u^{2} \sin ^{2} \alpha / g^{2}} \Rightarrow \dfrac{h}{t^{2}}=\dfrac{g}{8}$
$\Rightarrow h=\dfrac{g t^{2}}{8}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Two bodies were thrown simultaneously from the origin, one straight up and the other, at angle $60^{\circ}$ to the vertical. The initial velocity of each body is equal to $10 {~ms}^{-1}$. Neglecting the air resistance, the distance between the two bodies after $t=2 s$ is (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, bodies are thrown with speed $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$, $i.e.$ $u_{1}=u_{2}=10 {~ms}^{-1}$.
One is vertically up, $i.e.$ $90^{\circ}$ with horizontal, and other is thrown at angle $\theta$ with vertical, $i.e.$ $\theta=60^{\circ}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957776/preptracker/questions/q_107298_explanation_1764957776353.png Now, Let $S_{12}$ be the relative position of 1 with respect to 2 and $\vec{v}_{12}$ be the initial velocity of 1 with respect to 2.
Now, $\vec{v}_{1}=\vec{v}_{2}+\vec{v}_{12}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957777/preptracker/questions/q_107298_explanation_1764957777425.png Using triangle law,$v_{12}=\sqrt{v_{1}^{2}+v_{2}^{2}-2 v_{1} v_{2} \cos \theta}$
$=\sqrt{10^{2}+10^{2}-2(10)^{2} \times \cos 60^{\circ}}$
$=\sqrt{200-200 \times \dfrac{1}{2}}=\sqrt{100}=10 {~m} / {s}$
and $\quad v_{12}=\dfrac{S_{12}}{t}$
$\therefore \quad s_{12}=v_{12} t=10 \times 2=20 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is thrown at a speed which is twice its speed at its maximum height. If $R$ and $H$ are its range and maximum height respectively, then the ratio $\dfrac{R}{H}$ is
explanation:
Max.height $H = \frac{{u_1^2{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and $R = \frac{{u_1^2\sin 2\theta }}{g}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957774/preptracker/questions/q_107296_explanation_1764957774408.png $u_{2}=u_{1} \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow u_{2}=2 u_{2} \cos \theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \cos \theta=\cos 60^{\circ} \Rightarrow \theta=60^{\circ}$
On dividing Eq. (2) by Eq. (1), we get
$\dfrac{R}{H}=\dfrac{u_{1}^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g} \times \dfrac{2 g}{u_{1}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}$
$=\dfrac{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{\sin ^{2} \theta} \cdot 2=\dfrac{2 \cos \theta}{\sin \theta} \cdot 2=4 \cot \theta$
As, $\theta=60^{\circ}$
$\therefore \dfrac{R}{H}=4 \cot 60^{\circ}=4 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}=\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{3}}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is thrown in the upward direction making an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal with velocity of $140 {~m} / {s}$. Then, the time after which its velocity makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal is (Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Velocity of the projectile, $v=140 {~m} / {s}$
The angle of projection, $\theta=60^{\circ}$.
Then horizontal component of the velocity is always constant throughout the motion.
Let the velocity is be $\nu$ when the angle is $30^{\circ}$ with horizontal.
$\therefore \quad u \cos 60^{\circ}=v \cos 30^{\circ} {s}$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{u}{2}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} v}{2} \Rightarrow v=\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{3}}=\dfrac{140}{\sqrt{3}} {~m} / {s}$
For vertical motion, $u_{y}-v_{y}=g t$
$\Rightarrow u \sin 60^{\circ}-v \sin 30^{\circ}=g t$
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{3} u}{2}-\dfrac{140}{\sqrt{3}} \times \dfrac{1}{2}=g t$
$\Rightarrow g t=\dfrac{140 \sqrt{3}}{2}-\dfrac{140}{2 \sqrt{3}}=\dfrac{140}{2}\left(\sqrt{3}-\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{70(3-1)}{\sqrt{3} \times 10}=\dfrac{14}{\sqrt{3}} s \quad\left(\because g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is thrown into air with velocity $15 {~m} / {s}$ at an angle $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. After what time its direction of motion is perpendicular to its initial direction?(Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The given projectile motion is as shown in figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957773/preptracker/questions/q_107290_explanation_1764957772759.png Let $t$ be the time after which the initial direction is perpendicular to its direction of motion. As, velocity of a projectile has two components. So, its initial velocity is given by
${v_1} = u\cos \theta \hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
Since, in projectile motion the horizontal component of velocity remains same, while the vertical component varies, so its velocity after time $t$,
$v_{2}=u_{y} \hat{i}+v_{y} \hat{j}$
$v_{2}=u \cos \theta \hat{i}+(u \sin \theta-g t) \hat{j}$
$\left[ {\because {v_y} = {u_y} - gt = u\sin \theta - gt} \right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
As, velocities are perpendicular, so $v_{1} \cdot v_{2}=0$
From Eqs (1) and (2), we get
$\Rightarrow(u \cos \theta \hat{i}+u \sin \theta \hat{j})$.
$(u \cos \theta \hat{i}+(u \sin \theta-g t) \hat{j})=0$
$\Rightarrow u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta+u \sin \theta(u \sin \theta-g t)=0$
$\Rightarrow u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta+u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta-u \sin \theta \times g t=0$
$\Rightarrow u^{2}\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)-u g t \sin \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow u^{2}=u g t \sin \theta \quad\left(\because \sin ^{2} \theta+\cos ^{2} \theta=1\right)$
$\Rightarrow t=\dfrac{u}{g \sin \theta}$
Here, $u=15 {~m} / {s}, g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ and $\theta=30^{\circ}$
$t=\dfrac{15}{10 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}=\dfrac{15 \times 2}{10}=3 {~s}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A particle is projected from the ground with some initial velocity making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. If it reaches a height of $7.5\,m$ above the ground, while it travels a horizontal distance of 10 m from the point of projection, then the initial speed of particle is (assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
The given situation is as shown in figure, https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764957771/preptracker/questions/q_107289_explanation_1764957770715.png Let the particle reaches point $P$ in time $t$.
Then, horizontal distance,$x=u \cos \theta t \Rightarrow 10=u \cos 45^{\circ} \times t$
$ \Rightarrow t = \frac{{10\sqrt 2 }}{u}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and vertical distance,$y=u \sin \theta t-\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$ \Rightarrow 7.5 = u\sin {\rm{ }}45^\circ \times t - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times {t^2}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \left( 2 \right)$
Substituting value of $t$ from Eq. (1) into Eq. (2), we get
$\Rightarrow 7.5=\dfrac{u}{\sqrt{2}} \times \dfrac{10 \sqrt{2}}{u}-\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 \times\left(\dfrac{10 \sqrt{2}}{u}\right)^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 7.5=10-\dfrac{1000}{u^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow u^{2}=\dfrac{1000}{2.5}=400$ or $u=20 {~m} / {s}$
Hence, the initial speed of particle is $20 {~m} / {s}$.
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is launched from point $A$ of the given landscape with a water body as shown in the diagram. The launching angle is $15^{\circ}$. From the following, identify the right initial velocity of the projectile with which it will fall somewhere in between the points $C$ and $D$. [Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ].
explanation:
The given situation is shown below https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954843/preptracker/questions/q_107285_explanation_1764954843304.png According to given condition, range must be greater than $20\,m$ and less than $24.2\,m$.
$\Rightarrow 20 \leq R \leq 24.2$
$\Rightarrow 20 \leq \dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g} \leq 24.2$
$\Rightarrow 20 \leq \dfrac{u^{2} \times \sin \left(2 \times 15^{\circ}\right)}{10} \leq 24.2$
$\Rightarrow 200 \leq u^{2} \times \dfrac{1}{2} \leq 242\left(\because \sin 30^{\circ}=\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$
$\Rightarrow 400 \leq u^{2} \leq 484 \Rightarrow 20 {~m} / {s} \leq u \leq 22 {~m} / {s}$
$21 \cdot 5 \approx 22\,m/s$
so the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is fired at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Elevation angle of the projectile at its highest point as seen from the point of projection is
explanation:
The given situation is shown in the following figure
Angle of projection, $\theta=45^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952112/preptracker/questions/q_107282_explanation_1764952111905.png The elevation angle of the projectile at its highest point as seen from the point of projection is $\alpha$.
$\therefore$ In $\triangle P O M$,
$\tan \alpha=\dfrac{H}{R / 2}=\dfrac{\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}}{\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{2 g}}=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} \theta}{\sin 2 \theta}$
$=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} 45^{\circ}}{\sin 90^{\circ}}=\dfrac{(1 / \sqrt{2})^{2}}{1}$
$\Rightarrow \tan \alpha=\dfrac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A particle aimed at a target, projected with an angle $15^{\circ}$ with the horizontal is short of the target by $10\,m$. If projected with an angle of $45^{\circ}$ is away from the target by $10\,m$, then the angle of projection to hit the target is
explanation:
Let $d$ be the distance of target.
The horizontal range of projectile is
$R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
When, $\theta=15^{\circ}$
$R_{1}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin \left(2 \times 15^{\circ}\right)}{g}=\dfrac{u^{2}}{2 g}$
When $\theta=45^{\circ}$,
$R_{2}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin \left(2 \times 45^{\circ}\right)}{g}=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}$
According to question,
${R_1} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} = d - 10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
and ${R_2} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g} = d + 10\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
Dividing Eq. (1) by Eq. (2), we get
$\dfrac{1}{2}=\dfrac{d-10}{d+10} \Rightarrow d=30 {~m}$
From Eq. (2), $\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}=30+10=40 {~m}$
So, to hit the target at $30\,m$,
$30=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g} \Rightarrow 30=40 \sin 2 \theta \quad[$ from eq (3) $]$
$\Rightarrow \theta=\dfrac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right)$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A ball is projected with a velocity $5 {~m} / {s}$, so that its horizontal range is twice the greatest height attained. The value of range is
explanation:
Given $R=2 H$$\Rightarrow \dfrac{u^{2}}{g} \sin 2 \theta=2 \times \dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}$
$\Rightarrow \tan \theta=2$
$\Rightarrow \sin \theta=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$ and $\cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$
So, the horizontal range
$R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}=\dfrac{u^{2} \times 2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{g}$
$=\dfrac{(5)^{2} \times 2 \times \dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}} \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}}{10}=2 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: An archer shoots an arrow from a height $4.2\,m$ above the ground with a speed $40 {~m} / {s}$ and at an angle $30^{\circ}$ as shown in the figure. Determine the horizontal distance $R$ covered by the arrow, when it hits the ground, (Take $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, speed of arrow, $v=40 {~m} / {s}$ and $\theta=30^{\circ}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952101/preptracker/questions/q_107269_explanation_1764952101235.png $A B=$ Horizontal range $R_{1}$ covered by the arrow is given by
$R_{1}=\dfrac{v^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}=\dfrac{40^{2} \sin \left(2 \times 30^{\circ}\right)}{10}=160 \sin 60^{\circ}$
$=160 \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=80 \sqrt{3} {~m}$
If $t$ be the time taken by the arrow reaching from $B$ to $C$, then from the second equation of motion,
$\Rightarrow h=u t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
Hence, $h=4.2 m, u=v \sin 30^{\circ}$
$\therefore 4.2=v \sin 30^{\circ} t+\dfrac{1}{2} g t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 4.2=40 \times \dfrac{1}{2} t+\dfrac{1}{2} \times 10 t^{2} \Rightarrow 4.2=20 t+5 t^{2}$
$\Rightarrow 50 t^{2}+200 t-42=0$
$\Rightarrow 25 t^{2}+100 t-21=0$
$\Rightarrow(5 t+1)(5 t+21)=0$
$\therefore t=\dfrac{1}{5} s$
$D C=$ Distance travelled in horizontal direction is given by
$R_{2}=v \cos 30^{\circ} \times t=40 \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \dfrac{1}{5}=4 \sqrt{3} {~m}$
Hence, the horizontal distance $E C$ covered by the arrow,
$R=R_{1}+R_{2}=80 \sqrt{3}+4 \sqrt{3}=84 \sqrt{3} {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: A small object is thrown at an angle $45^{\circ}$ to the horizontal with an initial velocity $v_{0}$. The velocity is averaged for first $\sqrt{2} s$ and the magnitude of average velocity comes out to be same as that of initial velocity, $i.e.$ $\left|v_{0}\right|$. The magnitude $\left|v_{0}\right|$ will be (take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Trajectory of the particle is shown below. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952100/preptracker/questions/q_107265_explanation_1764952099743.png Let object is at $B(x, y)$ after $t=\sqrt{2} s$
Then, $x=u_{x} \times t=v_{0} \cos 45^{\circ} \times \sqrt{2}=v_{0}$
and $y=u_{y} t-\dfrac{1}{2} a_{y} t^{2}=v_{0}\left(\sin 45^{\circ}\right) \sqrt{2}$
$-\dfrac{1}{2}(10) \times(\sqrt{2})^{2}=\left(v_{0}-10\right) m$
Displacement $O B$ of particle is
$O B=\sqrt{O A^{2}+A B^{2}}=\sqrt{v_{0}^{2}+\left(v_{0}-10\right)^{2}}$
So, $v_{\text {avg }}=\dfrac{O B}{t}=v_{0}$ or $O B=v_{0} t=v_{0} \sqrt{2}$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{v_{0}^{2}+\left(v_{0}-10\right)^{2}}=v_{0} \sqrt{2}$
$\Rightarrow v_{0}^{2}+\left(v_{0}-10\right)^{2}=2 v_{0}^{2}$
$\Rightarrow v_{0}-10= \pm v_{0} \quad 2 v_{0}=10$
$\Rightarrow v_{0}=5 {~ms}^{-1}$
So, the correct option is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A cricket player can throw a ball with an $\max$.speed of $30 {~m} / {s}$. What is the maximum range of the ball upto which he can throw it?Neglect air resistance. [Take, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ ]
explanation:
Maximum range occurs, when angle of throw $\theta=45^{\circ}$
Also, $R_{\max }=\left.\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}\right|_{\theta=45^{\circ}}=\dfrac{4^{2} \sin ^{2} 2 \times 45^{\circ}}{g}$
$=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}=\dfrac{900}{10}=90 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (2).
subject: Physics

question: If a $1.5\,kg$ ball is shot obliquely at an angle of $34^{\circ}$ to the horizontal with an initial speed of $20 {~m} / {s}$, then maximum height achieved by the ball is (use $\cos 34^{\circ}=0.83$ or $\sin 34^{\circ}=0.56$ )
explanation:
Given, initial speed, $u=20 {~m} / {s}$
angle of projection, $\theta=34^{\circ}$
Maximum height, $H=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}$
$\Rightarrow H=\dfrac{(20)^{2} \times \sin ^{2} 34}{2 \times 9.8}=\dfrac{400 \times(0.56)^{2}}{2 \times 9.8} m$
$\Rightarrow H=\dfrac{125.44}{19.6}=6.3 {~m}$
So, the correct option is (1).
subject: Physics

question: The angle of projection for a projectile to have same horizontal range and maximum height is
explanation:
$H_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}$ $\quad \quad (1)$
$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$ $\quad $ $\quad $ $\quad \quad (2)$
As per question $H_{\text {max }}=R$
$\begin{aligned}& \dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g} ; \\& \Rightarrow \tan \theta=4 \\& \therefore \theta=\tan ^{-1}(4)\end{aligned}$
subject: Physics

question: A grasshopper can jump maximum distance 0.8 $m$ , while spending negligible time on the ground. How far  can it go in $3 {~s}\left({~g}=10 {~m} {~s}^{-2}\right)$ ?
explanation:
Maximum range $\left(\theta=45^{\circ}\right)=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}=0.8 \quad u=2 \sqrt{2} {~m}$
Distance covered in $3 {~s}=\left(4 \cos 45^{\circ}\right)(3)$
$=(2 \sqrt{2})\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)(3)=6 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question:
A man standing at the top of a building, throws several balls at different angles to the horizontal. All balls are thrown at speed $u=10 {~m} / {s}$ and it was found that all of them hit the ground making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ or larger than that with the horizontal. Find the height of the building.
(Take $\left.g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}\right)$
explanation:
Let $h$ be the height of the building.https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951184/preptracker/questions/q_33942_explanation_1764951184019.png In $y$-direction, $v_y^2 = u_y^2 + 2gh\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
In $x$-direction, ${{\rm{v}}_{\rm{x}}}{\rm{ = }}{{\rm{u}}_{\rm{x}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Also, $\tan \theta=\dfrac{v_{y}}{v_{x}}$
As per question minimum value of $\theta$ is $45^{\circ}$.
$\Rightarrow$ Ratio of minimum $v_{y}$ and maximum $v_{x}$ is $\tan 45^{\circ}=1$
$\left(v_{y}\right)_{\min }=\sqrt{2 g h}$ when $y$ component of initial velocity is minimum,$u_{y}=0$ and $\left(v_{x}\right)_{\max }=u$ when stone is projected horizontally.
$\therefore \dfrac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{u}=1 \Rightarrow \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 10 h}}{10}=1$, which gives $h=5 {~m}$
subject: Physics

question: A small ball is to be thrown, so as to just pass through three equal rings of diameter $2\,m$ and placed in parallel vertical planes at distance $8\,m$ apart with their highest points at height $32\,m$ above the point of projection as shown in the figure. if the angle of projection with horizontal is $\alpha .$ The value of $\tan \,\alpha $ is
explanation:
In figure, points ${A, B}$ and ${C}$ are lying on the trajectory of the path of the ball. The equation of trajectory is https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951175/preptracker/questions/q_33868_explanation_1764951175337.png $y=x \tan \alpha-\dfrac{g x^{2}}{2 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \alpha}$
For point ${A}$,
$32 - 2 = x\tan \alpha - \frac{{5{x^2}}}{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\alpha }}$
Or $30 = x\tan \alpha - \frac{5}{{{u^2}}}{x^2}\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
For point ${B}$
$32 = (x + 8)\tan \alpha - $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{5}{{{u^2}}}{(x + 8)^2}\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right){\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
For point ${C}$,
$32 - 2 = (x + 8)\tan \alpha - $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{5{{(x + 16)}^2}}}{{{u^2}}}\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right)$
$ \Rightarrow 30 = (x + 16)\tan \alpha - $
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{{5{{(x + 16)}^2}\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right)}}{{{u^2}}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 3 \right)$
Subtracting Eq. (1) from Eq. (2),
$2 = 8\tan \alpha - \frac{5}{{{u^2}}}(1 + \tan \alpha )$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{x^2} + 16x + 64 - {x^2}} \right)$
$ \Rightarrow \,\,\,2 = 8\tan \alpha - \frac{5}{{{u^2}}}\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right)$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {16x + 64} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 4 \right)$
Subtracting Eq. (1) from Eq (3),
$0 = 16\tan \alpha - \frac{{5\left( {1 + {{\tan }^2}\alpha } \right)}}{{{u^2}}}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( {{x^2} + 32x + 256 - {x^2}} \right)\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 5 \right)$
From Eq. (4) and Eq. (5)
${\tan ^2}\alpha = \frac{{32 \times 2}}{{64}} = 1\,$
$\therefore \,\,\,\,\,\,\tan \alpha = 1$
subject: Physics

question: The equation of motion of a projectile is $y=2 x-\dfrac{3}{4} x^{2}$. The horizontal component of velocity is $6\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ What is the range (in metres) of the projectile? (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
$y = 2x - \frac{3}{4}{x^2}$
$\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} - \frac{3}{4} \times 2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} - \frac{3}{2}x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
At $x = 0,\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}$
If $\theta$ is the angle of projection, then
$\frac{{\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}}}{{\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}}} = 2 = \tan \theta $
Using, $\sin ^{2} \theta=\dfrac{\tan ^{2} \theta}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}$ and $\cos ^{2} \theta=\dfrac{1}{1+\tan ^{2} \theta}$
$\therefore \sin \theta=\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$ and $\cos \theta=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{5}}$
If $u$ is the initial velocity of projection, then
$u \cos \theta=6$
$\therefore u=6 \sqrt{5}$
$\therefore \quad u \sin \theta=6 \sqrt{5} \times\left(\dfrac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)=12$
Horizontal range, $R = \frac{{2(u\sin \theta )(u\cos \theta )}}{g}$
$\therefore \quad R = \frac{{2 \times 12 \times 6}}{{10}} = 14.4\,m$
subject: Physics

question: When projected at an angle of ${15^{\circ}}$, the range of a projectile is $50\,m$ . If with the same speed, it is projected at an angle of ${45^{\circ}}$, its range will be
explanation:
Given : ${\theta_{1}=15^{\circ}, R_{1}=50 {~m}, \theta_{2}=45^{\circ}, R=}$ ? Range of a projectile is$R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\theta }}{g}$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} 50 = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 30^\circ }}{g}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {R_2} = {\text{ }}\frac{{{u^2}\sin {\text{ }}90^\circ }}{g}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Dividing eq. (1) by eq. (2), we get ${\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \,\frac{{50}}{{{R_2}}} = \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow {R_2} = 100\,m$.
So correct option is (2)
subject: Physics

question: A stone is projected from the ground with velocity $25\,m{\rm{/}}s$. Two seconds later, it just clears a wall $5\,m$ high. The angle of projection of the stone is $\left( {g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{{\sec }^2}} \right)$
explanation: $5 = 25\sin \theta  \times 2 - \frac{1}{2}g{\mkern 1mu} {(2)^2},\theta  = 30^\circ $
subject: Physics

question: A projectile projected at an angle $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal has a range $R.$ If the angle of projection at the same initial velocity be $60^{\circ}$, then the range will be-
explanation: If sum of angle of projection $=90^{\circ}$ for given speed then range for that angle of projection is same.
subject: Physics

question: Two projectiles $A$ and $B$ are thrown with the same speed but angles are $40^{\circ}$ and $50^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Then
explanation: $T=\dfrac{2 u \sin \theta}{\mathrm{g}}$, lesser is the value of $\theta$, lesser is $\sin \theta$ and hence lesser will be the time taken. Hence $A$ will fall earlier.

options.text: both will fall at the same time

subject: Physics

question: A stone projected with a velocity $u$ at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal reaches maximum height ${H_1}$. When it is projected with velocity $u$ at an angle $\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}-\theta\right)$ with the horizontal, it reaches maximum height ${H_2}$. The relation between the horizontal range $R$ of the projectile, heights ${H_1}$ and ${H_2}$ is
subject: Physics

question: What determines the nature of the path followed by the particle?
explanation:
The nature of path is decided by velocity
and acceleration.Velocity is ratio of displacement to time. It is a vector quantity possessing both
magnitude and direction. When velocity of a
particle changes, the particle is said to undergo
an acceleration.
The instantaneous acceleration is the derivative of the velocity with respect to time.
Acceleration is also a vector quantity, possessing both magnitude and direction. Depending on
variation of velocity and acceleration, the path
could be a straight line, circle or a parabola.
subject: Physics

question: Two bodies are thrown up at angles of $45^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ respectively, with the horizontal. If both bodies attain same vertical height, then ratio of velocities with which these are thrown is
explanation:
According to the question, heights of both
bodies are equal.
$\Rightarrow \dfrac{u_{1}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}{2 g}=\dfrac{u_{2}^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta_{2}}{2 g}$
or $\dfrac{u_{1}}{u_{2}}=\dfrac{\sin \theta_{2}}{\sin \theta_{1}}=\dfrac{\sin 60^{\circ}}{\sin 45^{\circ}}=\dfrac{\sqrt{3} / 2}{1 / \sqrt{2}}=\sqrt{\dfrac{3}{2}}$
subject: Physics

question: Two stones are projected with same velocity $v$ at an angle $\theta$ and $\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$.If $H$ are $H_{1}$ are the greatest heights in the two paths, then what is the relation between $R, H$ and $H_{1}$ ?
explanation:
Range of projectile,
$R = \frac{{2{u^2}\sin \theta \cos \theta }}{g}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1){\rm{ }}$
Height $H = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Also, ${H_1} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\left( {90^\circ - \theta } \right)}}{{2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(3)$
$ = \frac{{{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{2g}}{\rm{ }}$
${\rm{Then}},{\rm{ }}H{H_1} = \frac{{{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta {u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{2g \times 2g}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(4)$
From Eq. (1), we get
${R^2} = \frac{{4{u^2}{{\sin }^2}\theta {u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta \times 4}}{{2g \times 2g}}$
$R = \sqrt {16H{H_1}} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} [{\rm{from}}\,\,{\rm{Eqs}},({\rm{4}})]$
$ \Rightarrow R = 4\sqrt {H{H_1}} $
subject: Physics

question:
Read Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option given below.

Assertion: Generally, the path of a projectile from the earth is parabolic but it is elliptical for projectile going to a very large height.

Reason: The path of a projectile is independent of the gravitational force of the earth.
explanation:
Upto ordinary heights, the change in the
distance of a projectile from the centre of the
earth is negligible compared to the radius of the earth.
Hence, the projectile moves under a nearly
uniform gravitational force and its path is
parabolic. But for the projectiles moving up to a
large height, the gravitational force decreases
and hence the path of the projectile becomes elliptical.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

subject: Physics

question: A particle of mass $m$ is projected with a velocity $v$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal. When the particle is at its maximum height, the magnitude of its angular momentum about the point of projection is
explanation:
Maximum height, $H=\dfrac{v^{2} \sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}}{2 g}$
$ = \frac{{{v^2}}}{{2g}} \times \frac{3}{4}\left( {\because {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} \sin 60^\circ = \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2}} \right)$
$ = \frac{{3{v^2}}}{{8g}}$
Momentum of particle at highest point,
$p=m v \cos 60^{\circ}=\dfrac{m v}{2}$
Angular momentum $=p H$
( $\because$ angle between $P$ and $H$ is $90^\circ \,)$
$ = \frac{{mv}}{2} \times \frac{{3{v^2}}}{{8g}} = \frac{{3m{v^3}}}{{16g}}$
subject: Physics

question: The equation of trajectory of a projectile is $y=10 x-\left(\dfrac{5}{9}\right) x^{2}$. If we assume $g=10 m s^{-2}$, then the range of projectile (in metre) is
explanation:
Equation of projectile,
$y = 10x - \left( {\frac{5}{9}} \right){x^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
Standard equation of trajectory is
$y = x\tan \theta - \frac{g}{{2{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta }} \cdot {x^2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
On comparing, Eq. (1) and (2), we get
$\tan \theta=10$ and $\dfrac{g}{2 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta}=\dfrac{5}{9}$
or $10 u^{2} \cos ^{2} \theta=9 g$
$ \Rightarrow {u^2}{\cos ^2}\theta = 9\quad \left( {g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}} \right)$
Range of projectile,
$R=\dfrac{2 u^{2} \sin \theta \cos \theta}{g}=\dfrac{2 u^{2} \tan \theta \cos ^{2} \theta}{g}$
$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad (\sin \theta = \tan \theta \cos \theta )$
$ = \frac{{2\left( {{u^2}{{\cos }^2}\theta } \right) \cdot \tan \theta }}{g}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 9 \times 10}}{{10}} = 18\,m$
subject: Physics

question: The initial speed of a projectile fired from ground is $u$. At the highest point during its motion, the speed of projectile is $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} u$. The time of flight of the projectile is
explanation:
The horizontal component of speed $u$ is $u \cos \theta$.
So $u_{x}=u \cos \theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} u$
or $\cos \theta=\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \Rightarrow \theta=30^{\circ}$
Time of flight, $T=\dfrac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}$
$T=\dfrac{2 u \sin 30^{\circ}}{g}=\dfrac{2 u \times \dfrac{1}{2}}{g}=\dfrac{u}{g}$
subject: Physics

question: The range of the projectile projected at an angle of $15^{\circ}$ with horizontal is $50\,m$. If the projectile is projected with same velocity at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with horizontal, then its range will be
explanation:
$R=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g} \sin 2 \theta$
$\therefore \dfrac{R_{45}}{R_{15}}=\dfrac{\sin 2 \times 45^{\circ}}{\sin 2 \times 15^{\circ}}$
$\Rightarrow R_{45}=50 \times \dfrac{1}{1 / 2} m$
$ = 100\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Two projectiles are projected at $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal with the same speed. The ratio of the maximum height attained by the two projectiles respectively is
explanation:
Maximum height attained by projectile,
$H_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} \theta}{2 g}$
$\dfrac{H_{1}}{H_{2}}=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} \theta_{1}}{\sin ^{2} \theta_{2}}=\dfrac{\sin ^{2} 30^{\circ}}{\sin ^{2} 60^{\circ}}=\dfrac{1}{3}$
subject: Physics

question: The trajectory of projectile, projected from the ground is given by $y=x-\dfrac{x^{2}}{20}$. Where $x$ and $y$ are measured in meter. The maximum height attained by the projectile will be
explanation:
$y=x-\dfrac{x^{2}}{20}$
$\Rightarrow x^{2}-20 x+20 y=0$
for all real values of $x$,
$(-20)^{2}-4 \times 120 y \geq 0$
$\Rightarrow y \leq 5 \quad \therefore$ maximum height attained $\left( {{y_{\max }}} \right) = 5\,m$
subject: Physics

question: Two projectiles $A$ and $B$ are thrown with initial velocities of $40\,m{\rm{/}}s$ and $60\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at angles $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal respectively. The ratio of their ranges respectively is $\left( {g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}} \right)$
explanation:
Given:
${u_A} = 40\,m{\rm{/}}\sec ,{u_B} = 60\,m{\rm{/}}\sec $,
$\theta_{A}=30^{\circ} ; \theta_{B}=60^{\circ}$
Range of projectile, $R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
$\dfrac{R_{A}}{R_{B}}=\dfrac{(40)^{2} \sin 60^{\circ}}{(60)^{2} \sin 120^{\circ}}=\dfrac{16 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{36 \times \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}=\dfrac{4}{9}$
subject: Physics

Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct options given below:

Assertion: When a body is projected at an angle $45^{\circ}$, its range is maximum.

Reason: For maximum range, the value of $\sin 2 \theta$ should be equal to one.
explanation:
On a horizontal ground, projectile
$R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$ for $R_{\max }=\sin (2 \theta)=1 \Rightarrow \theta=45^{\circ}$

question: A projectile is projected at $30^{\circ}$ from horizontal with initial velocity $40\,m{s^{ - 1}}$. The velocity of the projectile at $t=2 s$ from the start will be(Given $g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$)
explanation:
$\vec u = \left( {40\,\,\cos 30^\circ \,\hat i + 40\,\,\sin 30^\circ \,\hat j} \right){\mkern 1mu} \,m/s$
$ = (20\sqrt 3 \hat i + 20\hat j)m{\rm{/}}s$
$\vec g = - 10\hat j,\,\,\,{\mkern 1mu} {\kern 1pt} t = 2\,{\rm{sec}}$
Now, $\vec{v}=\vec{u}+\vec{g} t=(20 \sqrt{3} \hat{i}+20 \hat{j})+(-10 \hat{j}) \times 2$
$ \Rightarrow \vec v = 20\sqrt 3 \hat i\,m{\rm{/}}s$
$\therefore \vec v = 20\sqrt 3 \,m{\rm{/}}s$.
subject: Physics

question: The maximum vertical height to which a man can throw a ball is $136\,m$. The maximum horizontal distance upto which he can throw the same ball is
explanation:
Maximum vertical height up to which a person can throw a ball Vertically is
$H_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2}}{2 g}$ where $u=$ Initial speed
$ \Rightarrow {H_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{{2g}} = 136\,m\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 1 \right)$
When the ball is projected making an angle $\theta$ with horizontal, the range on the ground is given by
$R=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
When $\theta=45^{\circ}$,
$ \Rightarrow {R_{\max }} = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( 2 \right)$
From (1) and (2), we get
${R_{\max }} = 2{H_{\max {\rm{ }}}} = 2 \times 136 = 272\,m$
So option (4) is correct.
subject: Physics

Reason: Air resistance affect the motion of the projectile.
explanation: Air resistance (or drag) can cause a projectile's path to deviate from the idealized parabolic trajectory predicted in the absence of air resistance. So correct option is (1).

explanation: The shape of an object's trajectory during motion is influenced not only by its acceleration but also by the initial conditions of motion, including initial position and initial velocity. So correct option is (3).

question:
Assertion: Generally the path of a projectile from the earth is parabolic but it is elliptical for projectiles going to a very great height.

Reason: Up to ordinary height the projectile moves under a uniform gravitational force, but for great heights, projectile moves under a variable force.
explanation: For ordinary heights, the change in a projectile's distance from the Earth's center is negligible compared to the Earth's radius. As a result, the projectile primarily experiences a nearly uniform gravitational force, following a parabolic trajectory. However, for projectiles reaching substantial heights, the gravitational force diminishes inversely proportional to the square of the projectile's distance from the Earth's center. In such cases, under this variable force, the projectile traces an elliptical path. So correct option is (1).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

explanation: To account for the vertical deflection of a bullet due to gravity, a marksman should aim the rifle higher than the target. The bullet undergoes a downward deflection of $(1/2)g{t^2}$ due to gravity. So correct option is (3).

So correct option is (3).

question: A stone is projected from ground. Its path is as shown in figure. At which point its speed is decreasing at fastest rate?

question: The velocity of a projectile at the initial point \(A\) is \[(2\hat i + 3\hat j)\,m{\rm{/}}s\]. Its velocity (in \[m{\rm{/}}s\] ) at point \(B\) is
explanation:

question:
Assertion: The maximum height of projectile is always $25 \%$ of the maximum range

Reason: For maximum range, projectile should be projected at $90^{\circ}$
explanation:
To obtain maximum range, angle of projection must be $45^{\circ}$, i.e. $\theta=45^{\circ}$.
$R_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin \left(2 \times 45^{\circ}\right)}{g}=\dfrac{u^{2}}{g}$
$\because H_{\max }=\dfrac{u^{2} \sin ^{2} 45^{\circ}}{2 g}=\dfrac{u^{2}}{4 g}=\dfrac{R_{\max }}{4}$
So, $H_{\max }$ is $25 \%$ of $R_{\max }$.

question: A projectile can have the same range $R$ for two angles of projection. If $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$ be the times of flights in the two cases, then the product of the two times of flights is proportional to

$\Rightarrow t_{1} t_{2}=\dfrac{2 R}{g}$
$\Rightarrow t_{1} t_{2} \propto R$

question: A particle is projected with speed $u$ that makes an angle  $\theta $ with horizontal, the time at which the velocity of the projectile is perpendicular to the initial velocity is
explanation:
The initial velocity vector is
$\overrightarrow u = u\cos \theta \hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j$
The velocity vector after time $t$ is
$\overrightarrow v = u\cos \theta \hat i + \left( {u\sin \theta - gt} \right)\hat j$
When $\overrightarrow v $ is perpendicular to $\overrightarrow u $
$\overrightarrow v .\overrightarrow u = 0$
${u^2}{\cos ^2} + {u^2}{\sin ^2}\theta - u\sin \theta gt = 0$
$t = \frac{u}{{g\sin \theta }}$
subject: Physics

question: A student is able to throw a ball vertically to maximum height of 40 $m$. The maximum distance to which the student can throw the ball in the horizontal direction is

question: A projectile is projected at an angle $\left( {\alpha  > 45^\circ } \right)$with an initial velocity $u$. The time ($t$) at which horizontal component will be equal the vertical component
explanation:
Let the angle be $\alpha $, then at any time ‘$t$’ in $x$ - direction,${v_x} = u\cos \alpha ,$ in $y$ - direction,
${v_y} = u\sin \alpha - gt$
${v_x} = {v_y}, \Rightarrow u\sin \alpha - gt = u\cos \alpha $
$ \Rightarrow u\left( {\sin \alpha - \cos \alpha } \right) = gt \Rightarrow t = \frac{u}{g}\left( {\sin \alpha - \cos \alpha } \right)$
subject: Physics

question: A bullet is fired with a speed $1000\,m/s$ of  in order to hit a target 100 $m$ away. If $g = 10m/{s^2}$, the gun should be aimed
explanation:
Let $h$ be the height of the target and the projectile is aimed at the top of the the height $h$ that makes an angle $\theta $ with the horizontal direction.
$R = \frac{{{u^2}}}{g}\sin 2\theta \Rightarrow \sin 2\theta = \frac{{100 \times 10}}{{{{\left( {{{10}^3}} \right)}^2}}} = \frac{1}{{1000}}$
$\therefore \theta \simeq \frac{1}{{2000}}\left[ {\theta \,{\mathop{\rm is}\nolimits} \,very\,small} \right]$
$\therefore \frac{h}{R} = \theta \Rightarrow h = \frac{{100}}{{2000}}m = 5\,cm.$
subject: Physics

question: A projectile is thrown with a velocity of $10\sqrt 2 \,m{s^{ - 1}}$ at angle  $45^\circ $ with horizontal. The interval between the moments when speed is $\sqrt {125} \,m{s^{ - 1}}$ is (Take $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$)
explanation:
Let the speed of the projectile be $\sqrt {125} m{s^{ - 1}}$ at time $t$. Then
${v^2} = v_x^2 + v_y^2$
Here,
${v_x} = {u_x} = u\cos 45^\circ = 10\sqrt 2 \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} = 10\,m{s^{ - 1}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (1)$
and ${v_y} = {u_y} - gt$
${v_y} = u\sin 45^\circ - 10t$
$ = 10\sqrt 2 \times \frac{1}{{\sqrt 2 }} - 10t = 10 - 10t{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} (2)$
Squaring and adding (1) and (2), we get
$v_x^2 + v_y^2 = {\left( {10} \right)^2} + {\left( {10 + 10t} \right)^2}$
${\left( {\sqrt {125} } \right)^2} = {\left( {10} \right)^2} + {\left( {10 + 10t} \right)^2}$
or ${\left( {10 + 10t} \right)^2} = 125 - 100 = 25$
$ \Rightarrow \left( {10 + 10t} \right) \pm = 5$
Hence, ${t_1} = \frac{{10 - 5}}{{10}}$ and ${t_2} = \frac{{10 + 5}}{{10}}$
$\therefore {t_2} - {t_1} = \left( {\frac{{10 + 5}}{{10}}} \right) - \left( {\frac{{10 - 5}}{{10}}} \right) = 1s$
subject: Physics
