question: A boy standing on a moving truck throws a projectile such that he is able to catch it back after the truck has moved $100\,m$. If the truck is moving horizontally along a straight line with a constant speed of $30 {~m} / {s}$, at what speed (relative to the truck) must the projectile is thrown. (Assume, $g=10 {~m} / {s}^{2}$ )
explanation:
Given, velocity of truck, $v=30 {~m} / {s}$. time taken by truck to move a distance of
$100 m, t=\dfrac{100}{30} \Rightarrow t=\dfrac{10}{3} s$
If $v_{1}$ be the velocity of projectile in upward direction then time taken by the projectile to reach at maximum height is $t_{1}$, then
$0=v_{1}-g t, v_{1}=g t_{1}$
But, $t_{1}=\dfrac{t}{2}=\dfrac{\dfrac{10}{3}}{2}=\dfrac{5}{3} {~s}$
$\therefore v_{1}=g \times \dfrac{5}{3}=\dfrac{10 \times 5}{3}=\dfrac{50}{3} {~m} / {s}$
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A particle is projected from a stationary trolley. After projection, the trolley moves with a velocity $2 \sqrt{15} {~m} / {s}$. For an observer on the trolley, the direction of the particle is as shown in the figure while for the observer on the ground, the ball rises vertically. The maximum height reached by the ball from the trolley is $h$ meter. The value of $h$ will be
explanation:
${v_x} = v\cos 60^\circ = 2\sqrt {15} \,m{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \quad v = 4\sqrt {15} \,m\,{s^{ - 1}}$
$\therefore \quad {v_y} = v\sin 60^\circ $
$ = 4\sqrt {15} \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} = 2\sqrt {45} \,m{s^{ - 1}}$
${H_{\max }} = \frac{{v_y^2}}{{2g}} = \frac{{4 \times 45}}{{20}} = 9\,m$
So correct option is (3).
subject: Physics

question: Two towers $A B$ and $C D$ are situated at distance $d$ apart as shown in figure. $A B$ is 20 $m$ high and $C D$ is 30 $m$ high from the ground. A particle is thrown from the top of $A B$ horizontally with a velocity of $10 {~ms}^{-1}$ towards $C D$. Simultaneously, another particle is thrown from the top of $C D$ at an angle $60^{\circ}$ to the horizontal towards $A B$ with the same magnitude of initial velocity as that of the first object. The two particles move in the same vertical plane, collide in mid-air, calculate the distance $d$  between the towers.
explanation:
Both the particles move under gravity, hence relative velocity between the particles will remain constant, For two particles to collide, their relative velocity must be directed along the line joining them.
The relative velocity of the particle $C$ with respect to $A$,
$\vec{v}_{C, A}=\vec{v}_{C}-\vec{v}_{A}=\vec{v}_{C}+\left(-\vec{v}_{A}\right)$
In given figure $\vec{v}_{C, A}$ represents the relative velocity of the particle $C$
with respect to $A$. As $\left|\vec{v}_{C}\right|=\left|\vec{v}_{A}\right|$, hence the angle between $\vec{v}_{C}$ and $\vec{v}_{C . A}$
should be $30^{\circ}$. Hence $\angle C A E=30^{\circ}$. Now in $\triangle C A E$,
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764955691/preptracker/questions/q_33952_explanation_1764955690955.png
$\tan 30^\circ = \frac{{10}}{d} \Rightarrow d = \frac{{10}}{{\tan 30^\circ }}$
$ = 10\sqrt 3 \,m = 17.3\,\,m$
subject: Physics

question: A stone ${A}$ is thrown at an angle of ${45^{\circ}}$ to the horizontal Another stone ${B}$ is thrown with ${20 {~ms}^{-1}}$ horizontally as shown in figure. They collide in mid-air. If distance ${B C}$ is ${4 n}$ meter, then the value of ${n}$ is
explanation:
${v_{A}=u_{A}-g t \hat{j}}$ and ${v_{B}=u_{B}-g t \hat{j}}$
${\therefore v_{A B}=v_{A}-v_{B}=u_{A}-u_{B}}$
$ = 10\hat i - 10\hat j + 20\hat i = 30\hat i - 10\hat j$
$\therefore \tan \theta = \frac{1}{3}$ https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951127/preptracker/questions/q_33881_explanation_1764951127101.png It means relative velocity of ${A}$ with respect to ${B}$ is directed at ${\theta}$ with vertically downward direction.
For collision, ${v_{A B}}$ should be towards initial position of ${B}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764951128/preptracker/questions/q_33881_explanation_1764951127882.png ${\therefore \tan \theta=\dfrac{B C}{36}}$
${\therefore B C=36 \times \dfrac{1}{3}=12=4 n}$
${\therefore n=3}$
subject: Physics

question: A lift is going up with an acceleration ${2 {~ms}^{-2}}$. A stone is thrown upward from its floor making angle ${15^{\circ}}$ with horizontal with a speed of ${4 \sqrt{3} {~ms}^{-1}}$ with respect to lift.  Find the horizontal range (in meter) inside the elevator.
explanation:
Here, effected acceleration due to gravity is
${{g_{eff{\rm{ }}}} = 10 + 2 = 12\,m{s^{ - 2}}}$
${\therefore \,\,\,\,R = \frac{{{u^2}\sin 2\alpha }}{{{g_{eff{\rm{ }}}}}} = \frac{{{{(4\sqrt 3 )}^2}\sin 30^\circ }}{{12}}}$
${ = \frac{{48}}{{12 \times 2}} = 2\,m}$
subject: Physics

question:
A train is moving along a straight line with a constant acceleration a. A boy standing in the train throws a ball forward with a speed of $6\,m{\rm{/}}s$ at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. The boy has to move forward by $2.5\,m$ inside the train to catch the ball back at the initial height. The acceleration of the train is
(Take $\sqrt 3 = 1.73,g = 10\,m{\rm{/}}{{\rm{s}}^2}$)
explanation:
$t = T = \frac{{2u\sin \theta }}{g}$
$ = \frac{{2 \times 6 \times \sin 30^\circ }}{{10}} = 0.6\,s$
Displacement of train in time $t = \frac{1}{2}{a^2}$
Displacement of boy w.r.t. train $ = 2.5\,m$
$\therefore $ Displacement of boy w.r.t. ground $ = \left( {2.5 + \frac{1}{2}{\rm{a}}{{\rm{t}}^2}} \right)$
Displacement of ball w.r.t. ground $ = \left( {u\cos 30^\circ } \right)t$
For catching the ball back at intial height,
$2.5 + \frac{1}{2}{\rm{a}}{{\rm{t}}^2} = \left( {u\cos 30^\circ } \right)t$
$\therefore \quad 2.5 + \frac{1}{2}a{(0.6)^2} = 6 \times \frac{{\sqrt 3 }}{2} \times 0.6$
$\therefore \quad \frac{9}{{50}}a = 3 \times \sqrt 3 \times 0.6 - 2.5$
$\therefore \quad \frac{9}{{50}}a = 0.614$
$\therefore \quad a = \frac{{0.614 \times 50}}{9}$
$\therefore \quad a = 3.41\,m{\rm{/}}{s^2}$
subject: Physics

question: A cart is moving horizontally along a straight line with constant speed  $30\,m{s^{ - 1}}.$ A projectile is to be fired from the moving cart in such a way that it will return to the cart after the cart has moved  $80\,m.$  At what speed (relative to the cart) must the projectile be fired?(Take, $g = 10\,m{s^{ - 2}}$ )
explanation:
As seen from the cart, the projectile moves
vertically upward and comes back. The time
taken by cart to cover $80\,m,\,\,t = - \frac{s}{v} = \frac{{80}}{{30}} = \frac{8}{3}\,\,\,s$
For a projectile going upward
${a = - g = - 10{m^{ - 2}},v = 0}$
and ${t = \frac{{8/3}}{2} = \frac{4}{3}s}$
$\therefore $${v = u + at}$
$ \Rightarrow $${0 = u - 10 \times \frac{4}{3}}$
$ \Rightarrow {\rm{ }}u = \left( {\frac{{40}}{3}} \right)m{s^{ - 1}}$
subject: Physics

question: A projectile \(A\) is thrown at an angle \(30^{\circ}\) to the horizontal from point \(P\). At the same time, another projectile \(B\) is thrown with velocity \(v_{2}\) upwards from the point \(Q\) vertically below the highest point. For \(B\) to collide with \(A, \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}\) should be
explanation:
Both the projectiles will collide in the air if vertical component of velocity of projectile $A$ is equal to the velocity of projectile $B$.
$v_{1} \sin 30^{\circ}=v_{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{v_{2}}{v_{1}}=\dfrac{1}{2}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is projected from a trolley car with a velocity $\overrightarrow v $. If the trolley car moves with an acceleration towards right, which of the following remain unchanged relative to both ground and trolley car?
explanation:
Vertical component of velocity is unchanged relative to trolley and ground.
Now time of flight $ = \frac{{2v\sin \theta }}{g}$
Time of flight remains unchanged relative to trolley & ground. Hence right choice is (4).
subject: Physics

question: A person sitting in an open car moving at 	constant velocity throws a ball vertically up into 	air. The ball falls
explanation: Horizontal velocity of ball and person are same so both will cover equal horizontal distance in a given interval of time and after following the parabolic path the ball falls exactly in the hand which threw it up.

options.text: In the car ahead of the person
In the car to the side of the person
Exactly in the hand which threw it up

subject: Physics

question: A stone is  just released from the window of a train moving along a horizontal straight track. The stone will hit the ground following a:
explanation: The horizontal velocity of the stone will be the same as that of the train. Vertical motion is accelerated motion. Thus, the resultant motion will be along a parabolic path.
subject: Physics

question: $A$ and $B$ are two trains moving parallel to each other. If a ball is thrown vertically up from the train $A$, the path of the ball is

options.text: Parabola for an observer standing on the ground
Vertical straight line for an observer in  $A$
A parabola for an observer in $B$ when $B$ is moving with same speed but in opposite direction

subject: Physics

question: A car is moving along a road with a speed of 45 $km$/$hr$. In what direction must a body be projected from it with a velocity of 25 $m$/$s$, so that its resultant motion is at right angles to the direction of car?
explanation:
${v_c} = 45\,km/h = \frac{{25}}{2}m/s$
For the resultant motion to be upwards.
$v\cos \theta + {v_c} = 0$
$\cos \theta = - \frac{{{v_c}}}{v} = - \frac{{25/2}}{{25}} = - \frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow \theta = 120^\circ $

options.text: At an angle of  $120^\circ $ with the direction of motion of car
At an angle of  $60^\circ $ with the direction of motion of car
At an angle of  $90^\circ $ with the direction of motion of car
At an angle of $135^\circ $ with the direction of motion of car

subject: Physics

question: A bomber moving horizontally with 500 $m$/$s$ drops a bomb which strikes ground in 10$s$. The angle of strike with horizontal is
explanation:
$\tan \theta = \frac{{{v_y}}}{{{v_x}}} = \frac{{gt}}{{{v_x}}} = \frac{{10 \times 10}}{{500}} = \frac{1}{5}$
i.e., $\theta = {\tan ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{5}$ with horizontal
subject: Physics

question: A smooth square plate $ABCD$ is moving towards right with a uniform $v$. At what angle $\theta $ must a ball be projected from $A$ with speed $u$ so that it strikes the point $D$
explanation:
Ball will strike the point $D$ if velocity of particle with respect to platform is along $AD$ or component of its relative velocity along $AB$ is zero.
${\overrightarrow {\rm{v}} _b} = u\cos \theta \hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j$
${\overrightarrow v _{Plate}} = v\hat i$
${\overrightarrow v _{Plate}} = \left( {u\cos \theta - v} \right)\hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j$
So, $u\cos \theta = v$
or $\theta = {\cos ^{ - 1}}\left( {\frac{v}{u}} \right)$
subject: Physics

question: A boy projects a stone vertically perpendicular to the trolley car with a speed $v$. If the trolley car moves with a constant velocity $u$, the time of flight of the stone is:
explanation:
${v_0}\sin \theta = v\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(1)$
${v_0}\sin \theta = u\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,(2)$
Time of flight depends on vertical component of velocity.
$T = \frac{{2{v_0}\sin \theta }}{g} = \frac{{2v}}{g}$
subject: Physics

question: A particle is projected from a trolley car with a velocity  $\vec v.$ If the trolley car moves with an acceleration  ${\vec a}$ towards right, which of the following remain unchanged relative to both ground and trolley car?
explanation:
Vertical component of projectile relative to trolley, ${v_{yT}} = v\,\sin \theta $
And vertical component is unchanged relative to trolley and ground.
Now time of flight $ = \frac{{2v\sin \theta }}{g}$
Time of flight remains unchanged relative to trolley & ground. Hence right choice is (3).
subject: Physics

question: A smooth square plate of side length $L$ is moving  towards right with uniform speed $v$. A ball is projected from $A$ with speed $u$ that makes angle w.r.to horizontal. Find the time taken for the ball to strike $D$.
explanation:
The ball strikes $D$ if the velocity of the ball w.r.to the plank is along $AD$.
i.e., ${\overrightarrow v _b} = u\cos \theta \hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j$
${\overrightarrow v _b} = v\hat i$
${\overrightarrow v _{bP}} = \left( {u\cos \theta - v} \right)\hat i + u\sin \theta \hat j$
$ \Rightarrow u\cos \theta - v = 0$
$\cos \theta = \frac{v}{u}$
So time taken to reach $D$ is
$t = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L}{{u\cos \theta }}$
subject: Physics

question: A car starts from rest and accelerates at $5\,m/{s^2}$. At $t = 4s$, a ball is dropped out of a window by a person sitting in the car. What is the velocity and acceleration of the ball at $t = 6\,s$ ?  (Take $g = 10\,m/{s^2}$)
explanation:
${v_x} = at = 5 \times 4 = 20$
${v_y} = g\left( {\Delta t} \right) = 10\left( {6 - 4} \right) = 20$
$v = \sqrt {v_x^2 + v_y^2} = \sqrt {{{\left( {20} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {20} \right)}^2}} = 20\sqrt 2 $
subject: Physics
